list.subtitle: Capturing urban growth dynamics – A spatial approach
list.item: Africa has the fastest urban growth rate in the world – by 2050, Africa’s urban areas will be home to an additional 950 million people. This growth will see changes in the size, shape and functional features of the physical built-up area of urban agglomerations. In most cases, urban population growth is accompanied by the growth of the built-up area – through the construction of housing, industrial and commercial space, administrative and public services such as schools, hospitals and parks, and supporting infrastructure, the facilities and services that keep a city alive.

list.subtitle: Urban growth, merging and densification: The emergence of large urban areas
list.item: The city of Onitsha is located on the eastern bank of the Niger River in Nigeria's Anambra State and has a population of 1.1 million inhabitants (World Urbanisation Prospects, 2018). However, according to Africapolis data, the agglomeration of Onitsha is Africa’s third largest urban agglomeration with 8.5 million inhabitants.

Onitsha illustrates two features of African urbanisation: firstly, the fast pace of urbanisation dynamics – 15 years ago it did not figure in the continent’s top 50 largest agglomerations – and secondly, the central role of the growth of built-up areas in driving the formation of large agglomerations.

Its urban agglomeration spreads over an area 80 times larger than the city itself, extending over 100 kilometres with the historical center of Onitsha located on the extreme north. This huge, continuously built-up agglomeration is formed under the combined effects of growing urban areas, the merging of agglomerations, the absorption of smaller settlements and the densification of rural areas.

Onitsha emerged in a vast, densely populated area with several historic urban centres. Today, it englobes the cities of Aba, Nnewi, Owerri and Umuahia each with populations of more than 500,000 as well as many smaller ones like Ihiala and Nkwerre, all of which are experiencing their own growth.

However, one important condition for the formation of Onitsha is the very high rural density in its periphery and surrounding areas. This formerly scattered and linear settlement, whose expansion was previously constrained (naturally and/or politically), has, due to underlying demographic pressures, become so dense as to be considered urban. The vast agglomeration, now measuring approximately 2,900 km2, has multiple high-density centres linked by large areas with lower population densities whose urban character and form are not fully perceptible. However, the average density of the urban area is 3,000 inhabitants per km2, similar to some large metropolitan areas in Western Europe, such as Berlin (Germany) and Milan (Italy).

list.subtitle: An emerging urban reality
list.item: The Africapolis definition of “urban” focuses on the built environment, implying a strict distinction between urban and non-urban. While this appears to provide only limited information on functional aspects and ‘degree of urbanisation’, it does reveal some of the dynamics and challenges of urbanisation that will be increasingly important for managing and planning future trends.

The urban agglomeration of Onitsha is expected to reach 25 million inhabitants by 2050, and could merge in the process with Aba, Uyo and Port Harcourt to form a vast urban area of 50 million inhabitants. This example of Onitsha illustrates some of the trends that challenge common perceptions about urbanisation, such as the concept of rural exodus being a driver of urbanisation. Onitsha, for instance, has not grown through the influx of rural populations, but instead from the opposite – an increase in rural population density in existing settlements that have grown and merged into one bigger agglomeration as the density has reached a certain critical mass.

Onitsha is not an isolated case in Africa. There are many others in Cameroon, Ethiopia, Rwanda, and Uganda, for example, which highlight one of the urban realities of modern-day Africa that needs to be better understood and planned for. The challenge for citizens, policy-makers and researchers is to conceive and design investment, public service, planning strategies and administrative frameworks that are adapted to the reality of these urban areas.
